input.query_abstract: The Internet, as well as many other networks, has a very complex connectivity recently modeled by the class of scale-free networks. This feature, which appears to be very efficient for a communications network, favors at the same time the spreading of computer viruses. We analyze real data from computer virus infections and find the average lifetime and prevalence of viral strains on the Internet. We define a dynamical model for the spreading of infections on scale-free networks, finding the absence of an epidemic threshold and its associated critical behavior. This new epidemiological framework rationalize data of computer viruses and could help in the understanding of other spreading phenomena on communication and social networks.
input.hyp_generic_object: connectivity growth during random step-growth (branching) polymerization of multifunctional monomers
input.hyp_abstract: Step-growth polymerisation of multifunctional monomers builds macromolecules whose connectivity can, at a critical extent of reaction, span the entire sample as an infinite network - the gel point. Classical branching theory locates this point at a conversion set by the mean and variance of the monomer functionality distribution. Here we treat mixtures in which functionality is extremely polydisperse: most monomers bear two reactive groups, but a small, heavy-tailed population carries very many, so that the second moment of the functionality distribution diverges as the ensemble grows. Writing generating-function recursions for the probability that a randomly chosen bond leads only to a finite branch, and stratifying monomers by functionality class, we show that the usual finite critical conversion collapses to zero. In such mixtures an incipient sample-spanning network forms at infinitesimal extent of reaction: no matter how few bonds have been made, a macroscopic connected component already exists, nucleated on and propagated through the rare high-functionality monomers. The weight-average degree of polymerisation diverges from the outset, and the sol fraction departs from unity with an anomalous power law in conversion. We verify these predictions by kinetic Monte Carlo on random monomer ensembles. The result implies that trace amounts of highly branched cross-linkers can abolish the gelation threshold entirely - a design principle for instantaneously percolating gels and a caution for the storage stability of reactive formulations.
input.candidate_abstract: We review the observations and the basic laws describing the essential aspects of collective motion -- being one of the most common and spectacular manifestation of coordinated behavior. Our aim is to provide a balanced discussion of the various facets of this highly multidisciplinary field, including experiments, mathematical methods and models for simulations, so that readers with a variety of background could get both the basics and a broader, more detailed picture of the field. The observations we report on include systems consisting of units ranging from macromolecules through metallic rods and robots to groups of animals and people. Some emphasis is put on models that are simple and realistic enough to reproduce the numerous related observations and are useful for developing concepts for a better understanding of the complexity of systems consisting of many simultaneously moving entities. As such, these models allow the establishing of a few fundamental principles of flocking. In particular, it is demonstrated, that in spite of considerable differences, a number of deep analogies exist between equilibrium statistical physics systems and those made of self-propelled (in most cases living) units. In both cases only a few well defined macroscopic/collective states occur and the transitions between these states follow a similar scenario, involving discontinuity and algebraic divergences.
